narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—putting your heart into law school dreams only to hear that a model sees a 53% chance of not passing the bar exam feels deeply unfair, and I'm here to walk you through why it landed there, based on your unique profile. The biggest signal for the model was your Year 3 decile of 2.0, which stood out as the strongest pull toward struggle, suggesting challenges in keeping up with tougher coursework as things ramped up. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile of 7.0, which also tugged firmly in the same direction, painting a picture of grades that started decent but didn't hold strong over time. Your middle family income added another layer, as the model saw it as less of a buffer against the hurdles ahead compared to higher supports. Then there's your LSAT score of 18.0, which influenced things toward difficulty, much like your undergraduate GPA of 2.7 that echoed a pattern of academic hurdles. Your background as a Black male factored in too, pulling the prediction downward in the model's view of bar passage odds, alongside choosing full-time study—which, while dedicated, the model linked to added pressures. The one bright spot was your gender, as being male nudged things slightly toward success, but it wasn't enough to outweigh the rest. Together, these pieces formed a story for the model of someone whose grades trended down, with test scores and circumstances stacking risks high enough to tip over 50% failure odds. It's not a judgment on your potential or grit—many turn patterns like this around with targeted support. You might consider bolstering those later-year grades or LSAT prep to shift the narrative next time. Hang in there; this is just one data-driven snapshot, not your whole story.
